metadata.subject: English Language
metadata.description: Activity book for sixth grade preparatory students covering vocabulary, grammar, and language skills

sections.title: Study Tip - Learning Vocabulary
sections.definition: A good way to learn new words is to put connected words in sets.
sections.explanation: Using a dictionary, check the meaning of any words you don't know. Write the words in the correct set. There are five words in each set.
sections.notes: Example of word sets: Fruit: apples, oranges, grapes, bananas, strawberries

examples.problem: Learning new vocabulary words
examples.solution: Group related words into sets to memorize them more effectively

sections.title: Study Tip - Words with Similar Meanings
sections.definition: Words with similar meanings are not always interchangeable. It is easier to use a word correctly if you know whether it is a verb, a noun or an adjective.
sections.explanation: Be careful with these words: hurt, sore, pain. Hurt is a verb. Sore is an adjective. Pain is a noun.
sections.rules: Hurt is a verb (past simple: hurt). Sore is an adjective. Pain is a noun - in singular, it usually has an article.
sections.notes: My back hurts today. My back is sore. I have a pain in my stomach.

examples.problem: Choose correct word: 'My back ____ today'

sections.title: Lesson 2: Past Continuous and Past Simple
sections.definition: We can use the past continuous and the past simple in the same sentence to show that one action happened in the middle of another action.
sections.explanation: We use the past continuous for the longer action and the past simple for the shorter action.
sections.rules: Use 'while' and 'as' with the past continuous tense. Use 'when' or 'and' with the past simple tense.
sections.notes: I was ice skating, and I fell over.

examples.problem: While Ali (have) a shower, somebody (knock) at the front door.
examples.solution: While Ali was having a shower, somebody knocked at the front door.

examples.problem: I (clean) my room, and I (find) £30 under my bed.
examples.solution: I was cleaning my room, and I found £30 under my bed.

sections.title: Lesson 3: Adjectives Ending in -ing or -ed
sections.definition: The -ed words describe the person who has the feeling. The -ing words describe what or who gives them that feeling.
sections.explanation: Don't forget the difference between frightening and frightened, boring and bored, interesting and interested.
sections.rules: Use -ed adjectives for feelings (bored, frightened). Use -ing adjectives for things that cause feelings (boring, frightening).

examples.problem: She is not very interested / interesting in fashion.
This book is very bored / boring.
examples.solution: boring (describes the book that causes boredom)

sections.title: Lesson 3: Adjectives Ending in -ful
sections.definition: Adjectives ending in -ful have only one 'l', e.g., beautiful, peaceful. The adverb ending is -fully, e.g., beautifully, peacefully.
sections.explanation: Write four more adjectives ending in -ful.

examples.problem: Marwa sings beautiful / beautifully.
This is a very peaceful / peacefully area.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Phrasal Verbs
sections.definition: With some phrasal verbs, noun objects can come before or after the preposition. With pronoun objects, they must come before the preposition.
sections.explanation: Turn off the light OR Turn the light off. BUT: Turn it off (NOT: Turn off it).
sections.rules: Noun objects: can come before or after preposition. Pronoun objects: must come before preposition.
sections.notes: Common phrasal verbs: give up, take up, try on, turn down

examples.problem: Can you / turn on / the television?
examples.solution: Can you turn on the television? / Can you turn the television on?

examples.problem: I've already / turned on / it.

sections.title: Lesson 4: Prefixes Meaning Not
sections.definition: To form the opposite of some adjectives we can put prefixes: un-, in-, im-, il-, ir-.
sections.rules: un- can go in front of any letter. im- can only go in front of m or p. il- can only go in front of l. ir- can only go in front of r.

examples.problem: Make opposite of: correct, moral, fair

sections.title: Lesson 5: Expressions of Quantity
sections.definition: some, any, a lot of, plenty of and enough are used with countable and uncountable nouns. a few and many are used only with countable nouns. a little and much are used only with uncountable nouns.
sections.explanation: Countable: a few apples, many apples. Uncountable: a little oil, much oil.
sections.rules: With countable nouns: a few, many, (too) many, not many. With uncountable nouns: a little, much, (too) much, not much. With both: some, any, a lot of, plenty of, enough.

examples.problem: How _____ food do we need? (much/many)
There were only _____ people at the party. (a few/a little)

sections.title: Lesson 6: Used To - Past Habits
sections.definition: Used to is used to talk about past habits or states that are no longer true.
sections.explanation: In questions and negatives, drop the 'd' (used becomes use).
sections.rules: Affirmative: used to + verb. Negative: didn't use to + verb. Question: Did + subject + use to + verb?
sections.notes: Did you use to fight with your brother? What did you use to fight about?

examples.problem: Which TV programmes (you/watch) when you were little?
We (not sit) around watching a box!

sections.title: Lesson 7: Linking Words
sections.definition: Linking words connect ideas and sentences to create coherent text.
sections.explanation: Use linking words like: Firstly, Furthermore, In addition, Last but not least, As a result, Because, In my opinion.
sections.notes: Common linking words: firstly, secondly, furthermore, in addition, as a result, because, in my opinion

examples.problem: Complete: In my _____, everyone should go jogging.

sections.title: Lesson 9: Against All Odds - Najla's Story
sections.definition: Reading comprehension about Najla, an Iraqi Paralympic table tennis player who overcame adversity.
sections.explanation: Najla lost her legs in a bomb attack but became a champion table tennis player with prosthetic limbs.
sections.notes: Key vocabulary: prostheses, Paralympic, exoskeleton, physical therapy

examples.problem: What happened to Najla as a result of the bomb attack?
examples.solution: She lost her legs and had to learn to live in a wheelchair.

examples.problem: Why did the prostheses help Najla play better?
examples.solution: They allowed her to play in a different position and hold the racket properly.

sections.title: Revision: Phrasal Verbs
sections.definition: Review of common phrasal verbs and their usage.
sections.explanation: Phrasal verbs: carry on, find out, give up, look after, pick up, turn off, turn on.
sections.rules: carry on = continue, find out = discover, give up = quit, look after = take care of, pick up = collect, turn off/on = switch off/on

examples.problem: Dad, can you _____ me from football practice today?
I need you to _____ your little sister for a couple of hours.

sections.title: Revision: Past Continuous vs Past Simple
sections.definition: Review of using past continuous and past simple together.
sections.explanation: Use past continuous for longer background action, past simple for shorter completed action that interrupts.
sections.rules: Structure: While + past continuous, past simple. OR Past simple + when + past continuous.
sections.notes: Yesterday, I was skateboarding with my friends when I had a small accident.

examples.problem: Yesterday, I (skateboard) with my friends, when I (have) a small accident.
examples.solution: Yesterday, I was skateboarding with my friends, when I had a small accident.

sections.title: Revision: Expressions of Quantity
sections.definition: Review of quantifiers and which nouns they can be used with.
sections.explanation: much/many, a few/a little, some/any, a lot of/plenty of/enough.
sections.rules: much + uncountable, many + countable, a few + countable, a little + uncountable
sections.notes: Table: Only countable nouns - many, a few. Only uncountable nouns - much, a little. Both - some, any, a lot of, plenty of, enough.

examples.problem: Hurry up. There isn't _____ time before the bus leaves. (much/many)
Can you lend me a _____ money? (few/little)

sections.title: Revision: Negative Prefixes
sections.definition: Review of prefixes that form opposites: un-, in-, im-, il-, ir-.
sections.explanation: Complete words with correct prefix: il-, im-, un-, in-.
sections.rules: il- before l, im- before m/p, ir- before r, un- and in- for others.

examples.problem: Don't be _____ (polite).
It's _____ (legal) to sell cigarettes to teenagers.

sections.title: Writing Assignment: Cigarette Advertising
sections.definition: Write 100-120 words on the topic: 'Cigarette advertising should be illegal.'
sections.explanation: Consider these questions: How harmful are cigarettes? Where can advertisements be seen? Who sees them? What message do they give? Are young people easily influenced?
sections.rules: Essay should be 100-120 words. Include arguments for why cigarette advertising should be banned.
sections.notes: Consider: health effects on smokers and passive smokers, influence on young people, misleading advertisements showing attractive people smoking
